summarization.a: Representative rare earth ALD precursor ligand families are shown with example structures (diketonates, cyclopentadienyls, amidinate based, guanidinates, alkoxides, silylamides, acetamidinates, formamidinates). Colored squares indicate which rare earth elements have reported ALD use for each class.
summarization.b: Periodic table map of the extended rare earth elements (Sc, Y, La to Lu) used as the color key for the element coding in (a).

b.question: What is the purpose of grouping rare earth precursors by ligand class in this figure?
b.answer: The grouping highlights how precursor design for rare earth ALD and ALD/MLD is commonly discussed in terms of the coordinating ligand family, because the ligand controls volatility, thermal stability, and surface reaction behavior. By showing multiple classes side by side, the figure provides a quick overview of what chemistries have been used successfully across the rare earth series. The colored squares then connect each class to specific rare earth elements for which ALD use has been reported, helping readers see coverage and gaps.

b.question: In (b), are Sc, Y, and the lanthanides (La through Lu) the elements treated as the extended rare earth set in this review figure?

a.question: In (a), which precursor classes shown are nitrogen coordinated ligand families (rather than oxygen coordinated ones)?
a.answer:
Silylamides

, Acetamidinates

, Formamidinates

, Guanidinates

, Acetamidinate bis cyclopentadienyls (contains an amidinate plus cyclopentadienyl ligands)

a.question: How do the R′ and R″ substituents shown in (a) relate to practical precursor behavior in ALD?
a.answer: The substituents tune steric bulk and intermolecular interactions, which can shift melting point and volatility and can also influence surface reaction rates, so changing R′ and R″ is a standard way to adjust deliverability and reactivity of a precursor without changing the metal center.
